name: Operation Wide Receiver
description: Operation Wide Receiver was a controversial Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) operation launched in 2006 and shut down in 2011. The stated goal was to track firearms purchased illegally in the United States, particularly in Arizona, and smuggled into Mexico, in order to disrupt and dismantle Mexican drug cartels. The controversial aspect of the operation involved allowing straw purchasers to buy guns and transport them across the border without interdiction, with the hope of eventually tracing them back to high-level cartel members. Proponents within the ATF argued that this "gunwalking" strategy was a necessary evil to achieve a larger objective. Critics, including members of Congress and some ATF agents, contend that the operation was deeply flawed, dangerous, and ultimately counterproductive. They argue that it allowed thousands of weapons to fall into the hands of criminals, contributing to violence in Mexico and potentially even the United States. Mainstream perspectives typically view the operation as a significant failure of law enforcement, citing poor planning, inadequate oversight, and a lack of coordination between agencies. Investigations by the Department of Justice and Congress revealed serious shortcomings in the ATF's management and execution of the operation. The controversy surrounding Wide Receiver fueled debates about gun control, border security, and the effectiveness of U.S. efforts to combat drug trafficking.
subtitle: Controversial ATF Gunwalking Operation

claims.claim: Operation Wide Receiver allowed weapons to be trafficked into Mexico knowingly.
claims.evidence: ATF documents and whistleblower testimonies confirm that agents were instructed not to interdict straw purchasers.
claims.criticism: ATF officials claimed that the 'gunwalking' strategy was intended to track weapons to high-level cartel members, not to arm criminals. However, the lack of effective tracking and interdiction is widely acknowledged.

claims.claim: The operation contributed to violence in Mexico.
claims.evidence: Some weapons recovered at Mexican crime scenes were traced back to Operation Wide Receiver.
claims.criticism: While some weapons were traced back, the exact impact of Wide Receiver on overall violence levels in Mexico is debated, as many other sources of firearms exist.

claims.claim: High-ranking ATF officials were aware of and approved the 'gunwalking' strategy.
claims.evidence: Internal ATF emails and testimonies suggest a degree of awareness and approval from supervisors.
claims.criticism: The extent of knowledge and approval among top officials remains a point of contention, with some denying direct involvement or knowledge of the specific tactics used.

keyFigures.name: William Newell
keyFigures.role: ATF Special Agent in Charge, Phoenix Field Division during Operation Wide Receiver

keyFigures.name: Rep. Darrell Issa
keyFigures.role: Chairman of the House Oversight Committee, investigated Operation Wide Receiver

keyFigures.name: Sharyl Attkisson
keyFigures.role: Investigative journalist who reported extensively on Operation Wide Receiver

timeline.event: Operation Wide Receiver is shut down following media scrutiny and congressional inquiries.
The Department of Justice releases a report on Operation Wide Receiver, acknowledging serious errors and misjudgments.

sources.author: U.S. Department of Justice
sources.work: Review of ATF's Operation Wide Receiver
sources.title: U.S. Department of Justice
